plainMeaning: Next we bow our heads to the great teacher Himavanta, a man of boundless courage, endurance, and tireless study of the scriptures.
recitation: tatto himavaṁta mahaṁta vikkame dhii parakkama-m-aṇaṁte | sajjhāya-m-aṇaṁta dhare, himavaṁte vandimo sirasā ||

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit tatto < Sanskrit tatas (ablative of tad, 'from that'); idiomatically 'next in order'.
wordByWord.notes: Links this homage to the preceding teacher (Skandila), advancing the lineage roll one step.

wordByWord.gloss: Himavanta (the teacher); lit. 'the snow-possessing one', i.e. the Himālaya
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit himavant ('having snow') from hima ('snow') + possessive -vant; the proper name of Himavanta Khamāsamaṇa, simultaneously evoking the mountain.
wordByWord.notes: Object of homage; the name itself activates the Himālaya-simile of steadfastness.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit mahaṁta < Sanskrit mahant ('great'); qualifies Himavanta.
Prakrit vikkama < Sanskrit vikrama ('stride, prowess, heroism'); here ethical-spiritual prowess, in the locative of respect ('great in prowess').
wordByWord.notes: Doubled -kk- is the regular Prakrit assimilation of the conjunct -kr-.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit dhii < Sanskrit dhṛti ('firmness, constancy'); first member of the compound dhii-parakkama.
wordByWord.notes: Distinct from dhī ('intellect'); here the virtue of unshaken endurance, apt to the Himālaya image.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit parakkama < Sanskrit parākrama ('exertion, prowess'); compounded with dhii.

wordByWord.gloss: and endless / boundless (locative, qualifying his fortitude-and-valour)
wordByWord.etymology: Sandhi of an inserted euphonic -m- + aṇaṁta < Sanskrit ananta ('endless'); reads dhii-parakkama-m-aṇaṁte, 'of boundless fortitude and exertion'.
wordByWord.notes: The -m- is a hiatus-bridging consonant joining parakkama to aṇaṁte; the token is not a separate word but the sandhi'd 'aṇaṁte'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sajjhāya < Sanskrit svādhyāya ('self-study of scripture').
wordByWord.notes: A pointed word in a sajjhāy hymn: the lineage is praised for the very practice being performed.

wordByWord.etymology: Sandhi -m- + aṇaṁta < Sanskrit ananta; reads sajjhāya-m-aṇaṁta, 'of endless sacred study'.
wordByWord.notes: Again the euphonic -m- bridges sajjhāya and aṇaṁta.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit dhara < Sanskrit dhara ('holding') from root dhṛ ('to hold, sustain'); locative/accusative of respect, 'the upholder'.
wordByWord.notes: Casts Himavanta as one who carries and preserves the anuyoga, not merely studies it.

wordByWord.etymology: Same as himavaṁta, here in the case governed by vandimo.
wordByWord.notes: Resumptive repetition of the name closes the verse with the bow.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vandimo, 1st person plural < Sanskrit vandāmahe / vandāmaḥ from root vand ('to praise, salute').
wordByWord.notes: Plural 'we' — the assembled saṅgha bows together; the recurring homage-act of the hymn.

wordByWord.gloss: with the head, by bowing the head
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sirasā < Sanskrit śirasā, instrumental of śiras ('head'); the idiom śirasā vand- = 'to bow with the head'.
wordByWord.notes: Marks full bodily prostration, the most reverent form of vandanā.

literalTranslation: Then [to] Himavanta — great, of boundless fortitude and exertion in prowess, the bearer of endless sacred study — to Himavanta we bow with the head.
idiomaticTranslation: And then to Himavanta, mighty in valour, of fathomless fortitude and tireless striving, who upholds an endless treasury of sacred study — to him, Himavanta, we bow our heads in reverence.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having just bowed to Skandila, the poet's 'tatto' ('then') carries the roll one link onward to Himavanta Khamāsamaṇa, a guardian of the Kālika-śruta anuyoga; the next verse will pass to Nāgārjuna. The artistry of this gāthā is that the teacher's name is itself a simile. Himavanta means 'the snow-bearer' — the Himālaya — so every epithet that follows reads as both literal praise and mountain-imagery. He is 'mahanta' (great), like the great range; 'of boundless fortitude and exertion' (dhii-parakkama-m-aṇaṁte), as the Himālaya is immovable and unending; and the bow is 'sirasā', head lowered as before a peak. The hinge-word is sajjhāya: in a hymn that is itself recited as sajjhāy (svādhyāya), Himavanta is praised precisely as 'dhare', the bearer who holds 'endless sacred study'. He is honoured not for novelty but for custody — preserving the oral exposition intact and inexhaustible. The twin abstract nouns dhii (steadfast endurance) and parakkama (active striving) are deliberately paired: the lineage-guardian needs both the patience to hold and the energy to transmit. The verse closes by repeating the name — himavaṁte vandimo sirasā — folding subject and object into a single act of prostration, the plural 'vandimo' drawing the whole reciting assembly into the bow.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse crystallises the Jain ideal of the ācārya as śruta-dhara — a 'bearer of the scripture' whose merit lies in faithful preservation and transmission of the kālika-śruta, not in originality. Pairing dhṛti (fortitude) with parākrama (exertion) reflects the canonical understanding that guarding the teaching demands both endurance and energetic effort (cf. the saṁyama-vīrya, spiritual valour, prized in the Āvaśyaka and Uttarādhyayana traditions). 'Endless study' (sajjhāya-m-aṇaṁta) points to svādhyāya as one of the internal austerities (abhyantara-tapa), here raised to a lifelong, lineage-spanning discipline. The bodily bow (sirasā vandimo) enacts vinaya — reverence toward the knowledge-holder — which Jain ethics treats as a precondition for receiving knowledge at all.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: When chanting this verse, the reciter pauses on a single guardian and lets the Himālaya image steady the mind: study (sajjhāya) is to be approached with the mountain's two qualities — patient endurance and unflagging effort. The lowered head (sirasā) is a cue to physical humility: knowledge is received, not seized. It invites the practitioner to ask what scripture they themselves are 'bearing' forward.

crossReferences.text: Nandī-sūtra, Sthavirāvalī (Kālika-śruta anuyoga-dhara sequence: Skandila — Himavanta — Nāgārjuna)
śirasā vandāmi formula of reverential prostration
crossReferences.source: common Ardhamāgadhī vandanā idiom (e.g. Namutthuṇaṁ / Logassa traditions)

sources.title: Nandī Sūtra (Śvetāmbara Āgama) — text and translation
sources.consultedFor: Placement of Himavanta within the Nandī-sūtra Sthavirāvalī and the Kālika-śruta anuyoga-dhara succession (Skandila–Himavanta–Nāgārjuna).

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Root meanings of dhṛti (dhii), parākrama (parakkama), vikrama (vikkama), ananta (aṇaṁta), śiras/śirasā (sirasā), svādhyāya (sajjhāya).

sources.title: JainQQ digital scripture and paṭṭāvalī repository
sources.consultedFor: Sthavirāvalī / paṭṭāvalī tradition of scripture-transmitting ācāryas and the identity of Himavanta Khamāsamaṇa as a kālika-śruta guardian.

notes: The verse-final and -internal aṇaṁta tokens (maṇaṁte, maṇaṁta) carry a euphonic sandhi -m-; they are glossed as 'aṇaṁta' (endless), not as independent words. CAVEAT ON IDENTITY: some popular sources read 'himavanta' as an epithet of Bhadrabāhu (famed for Himālayan penance); per the tradition knowledge base and structure tree, in THIS hymn Himavanta is a distinct later teacher — Himavanta Khamāsamaṇa, a guardian of the Kālika-śruta anuyoga placed between Skandila (v.13) and Nāgārjuna (v.15) — whose proper name doubles as the Himālaya-simile of steadfastness; treat the name-equation as the tradition's. Original Gujarati script preserved verbatim (e.g. વિક્કમે, સજઝાય) without normalisation; case-endings of the locative/accusative of respect are rendered idiomatically.
